input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Now let me check available .NET SDKs and create a benchmark project.
data.reasoningText:
Now I need to:
1. Check what .NET SDKs are available
2. Set up a benchmark project using BenchmarkDotNet that targets both net9.0 and net10.0
3. Run the benchmarks

Let me check what's available and create the benchmark project.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "dotnet --list-sdks 2>&1 && echo \"---\" && dotnet --list-runtimes 2>&1", "description": "Check available .NET SDKs and runtimes", "initial_wait": 10}

data.content: Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. Let me create a BenchmarkDotNet project that targets both.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:
diff --git a/tmp/sv-ec7a9982b65e4dbda1ce1a713b0bf80d/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj b/tmp/sv-ec7a9982b65e4dbda1ce1a713b0bf80d/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-ec7a9982b65e4dbda1ce1a713b0bf80d/Benchmarks/Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,15 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: Let me fix the approach - I'll use the simpler multi-TFM configuration:
data.reasoningText:
The `CsProjCoreToolchain.NetCoreApp10_0` doesn't exist yet in this version of BenchmarkDotNet. Let me use a different approach - I'll use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute with runtime monikers or use the multi-TFM approach where BenchmarkDotNet automatically runs against each target framework.

Actually, the simplest approach is to just use `BenchmarkSwitcher` and let BenchmarkDotNet's built-in multi-TFM support handle this. When a project targets multiple frameworks, BenchmarkDotNet will run benchmarks for both.


I can simplify this further by just passing the runtime arguments directly when running the benchmarks - specifying `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0` will tell BenchmarkDotNet which runtimes to use.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't know `net10.0` yet. I'll run the benchmarks separately against each TFM:
data.reasoningText:
This version of BenchmarkDotNet doesn't have net10.0 runtime support. I'll need to use a different approach. Since the project targets both net9.0 and net10.0, I can run the benchmark separately for each framework. Let me use a simpler approach - just run it once under net9.0 and once under net10.0 and compare.

Actually, let me try a different approach. I'll use the `--runtimes net90` moniker (it's `net90` not `net9.0` in BenchmarkDotNet CLI args). But net10.0 isn't supported. 


I'll just execute the benchmark twice - once targeting net9.0 and once targeting net10.0, letting each run use its respective framework's runtime without specifying the `--runtimes` argument.
